# The current state

This report addresses the main points raised during the chat with @(Marat Akhin), to whom I am very grateful for being the initiator of this discussion.

To begin, a quick recap of what we're doing:
    * Explored ways to use Runtime Information (RI) generated by Kotlin applications with the idea to help LLMs get the better understanding of the application. We research whether we can solve some problems better, or even if we can solve some *new* set of problems. 
    * Outlined three main areas we want to focus on: *Mocking* (`mockLLM`), *Error Handling* (`catchLLM`) and *Data conversion* (`asLLM`). So far, we were focusing on *Mocking*. 
    * Discussed ways to interact with the LLM. So far we were focused on using LLM to produce code as the artifact.
    * Performed experiments on generating Mocks/Fakes on an open-source Spring project.
    * Runtime information is a “snapshot” of the actual behavior from some external services
    * We use mocks to “internalize” these external dependencies


## Quick Digression on Mocking:
The term Mocking is often incorrectly used by the developers. Me and Marat also had different understanding on what we percieve by mocks.
Let's use the [Martin Fowler's definition](https://www.martinfowler.com/articles/mocksArentStubs.html) as it seems to represent some kind of consensus.


    Dummy objects are passed around but never actually used. Usually they are just used to fill parameter lists.
    Fake objects actually have working implementations, but usually take some shortcut which makes them not suitable for production (an in memory database is a good example).
    Stubs provide canned answers to calls made during the test, usually not responding at all to anything outside what's programmed in for the test.
    Spies are stubs that also record some information based on how they were called. One form of this might be an email service that records how many messages it was sent.
    Mocks are objects pre-programmed with expectations which form a specification of the calls they are expected to receive.


Using these definitions:
    * Based on open-source projects, most Kotlin projects use mocking frameworks for dummies and stubs. There are rare cases of tests that use actual mocks.
    * Runtime Information seems to be promising in the context of Fakes and Mocks. The LLM can use RI to capture the current behaviour of the system. There are, however, challenges in updating Mocks/Fakes to correctly represent the up-to-date state of the system. One approach could be that the RI is provided online, or with a cronjob. This will keep the system and the Mocks/Fakes implementations synced. 
    * There is a caviat: If the RI is incomplete, LLMs are to interpolate the behaviour of the system, which is a complex task, and current experiments suggest that they fail at it. 

# The Decision Point

While experimenting with how the LLM interacts and uses RI, the following natural idea came to mind – *a more general MCP / Agent tool* for providing runtime information to LLMs
    * Observation: we can solve the original problem on top of this tool. On top of that, other developers might add upon this API for other features.
    * Observation: there are some teams internally that work on collecting Runtime information in JetBrains. They do not focus Kotlin though. The infromation they collect is also quite granular and focused more on catching errors.

We also discussed the following approach to deepen the `mockLLM` – Since sometimes there is not enough RI that is relevant for an interface, we might collect this RI once by using the *Forwarding* approach for LLMs (see previous posts). Then based on collected RI, we generate code for the Fakes/Mocks. The costs of this approach should be analyzed. Generally, it seems to be a good way to bootstrap the tests in general. 

The decision has to be made. Do we follow with 
    1) (IMO more exciting and ambitious) option of creating a MCP/Agent tool/etc that allows us to implement more features. There might be some overlap with other teams, although we can leverage this as a collaboration point and take some existing results to tailor them to our experiments. This is still relevant to Kotlin as we can design some API that will directly dump the RI to this tool.
    2) continue with Mocks/Fakes, and try the bootstraping idea. There is a risk that the results are less exciting.

@Stanislav Sandler: Option 1 seems more interesting and promising.
@Marat Akhin: We should ask @Simon Ogorodnik to check what aligns more with Kotlin's interests.
